title: Mustard on Silk — Glycerin + Minimal Detergent
stainChemistry: Mustard is ground mustard seeds (fat + protein) plus turmeric (dye). Fat component requires solvent; dye component responds to acid/glycerin. Silk cannot tolerate bleach or alkaline. Glycerin mobilizes both components while remaining gentle on protein fiber.
whyThisWorks: Glycerin (amphoteric) dissolves both fat and dye components. Minimal detergent encapsulates residue. No bleach risk on silk.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply glycerin from back side sparingly. Do NOT saturate silk. Allow 10-15 minutes. Blot gently as mustard mobilizes.

spottingProtocol.agent: Blot away glycerin and mustard residue
spottingProtocol.instruction: Blot gently. Do not rub. Yellow residue should transfer to cloth.

spottingProtocol.agent: NSD (1 teaspoon NSD in 1 cup cool water, minimal saturation)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply dilute detergent very sparingly. Do NOT saturate. Detergent encapsulates any remaining mustard particles for lifting.
Use barely damp cloth only. Do NOT spray or saturate. Work quickly—silk weakens at saturation.

homeSolutions.agent: Mustard on silk: turmeric is the real enemy
homeSolutions.instruction: Mustard's yellow color comes from turmeric (curcumin) — the same pigment behind curry stains and one of the hardest natural dyes to remove. On silk (protein fiber), you can't use the strongest turmeric tools: no enzymes, no alkaline, no oxidizer above very dilute peroxide for 45 sec. Fresh mustard within an hour, caught on light silk, is winnable with a glycerin loosener + gentle detergent. Aged or set mustard on silk is pro territory — expect partial removal even then.

Silk: no enzymes, no alkaline (ammonia, baking soda), no hot water, no alcohol, no wringing. Curcumin specifics: alkaline products turn yellow curcumin RED permanently (worse than the original stain). Sunlight breaks down curcumin — normally helpful, but prolonged sun exposure also fades silk dye, so keep sun exposure short and only on white/light silk.

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test + scrape off solids (no rubbing)
homeSolutions.instruction: Dab cool water on an inside seam, wait 60 seconds, blot. If the silk holds color, proceed. Lift off any mustard chunks with the edge of a plastic card or butter knife, scraping away from the center. Don't rub, don't press. Silk fibroin ruptures under wet friction.

Apply a small amount of glycerin (vegetable glycerin from pharmacy or craft store — under $5) directly to the stain. Let it sit 10–15 minutes. Glycerin softens and loosens the mustard's binding to silk fibroin without using any chemistry that would damage the fiber. Blot with a clean damp cloth — press, don't rub.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — cool, damp cloth only
homeSolutions.instruction: Mix one teaspoon of clear Dawn with two cups of cool water. Dip a white cloth, wring nearly dry, PRESS onto the stain. 5 minutes dwell. Blot. Repeat once. Dawn emulsifies the mustard's oil component (mustard contains mustard oil and often vinegar). On silk, press — never rub.

homeSolutions.agent: Very dilute peroxide — STRICT 45-sec, white silk only
homeSolutions.instruction: If yellow shadow remains AND the silk is white or very light AND your patch test was clean: mix 1 part 3% peroxide with 2 parts cool water. Dab a cotton swab for 30–45 SECONDS MAX, flush with cool water immediately. Skip on any colored silk. Curcumin is stubborn — even with peroxide, expect to see improvement rather than full removal. Do NOT extend dwell; silk yellows past 45 seconds.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never exceed 45 seconds peroxide on silk. Never combine with ammonia or bleach.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate to a silk specialist for residual yellow
homeSolutions.instruction: If the patch test failed, the mustard was older than a few hours, or yellow remains after step 6, take the garment to a silk specialist (not every cleaner — call ahead). Tell them: "Mustard (turmeric-based yellow) on silk, age [x]. Treated at home with glycerin + Dawn + [45-sec dilute peroxide on white]. No alkaline, no enzymes, no heat." Pros use silk-safe reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite) with controlled dwell — often the only home-adjacent chemistry that removes curcumin cleanly without fiber damage.

materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — silk dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
NEVER use hot water—spreads stain and weakens silk.
escalation: If glycerin and detergent don't fully resolve mustard, call professional. Do not attempt bleach or aggressive treatments.

household.name: Food-Grade Glycerin
household.note: Primary treatment. Apply minimally. Mobilizes both fat and dye.

household.name: Seventh Generation Free & Clear (diluted)
household.note: For final residue. Use very diluted. Minimal application.

professional.name: Specialty Silk Treatment
professional.note: For aged or stubborn mustard on silk.

customerExplanation: Mustard is fat plus turmeric dye. Glycerin mobilizes both components without risking bleach or alkaline damage to your delicate silk. This is a gentle multi-step approach.
scienceNote: Mustard's fat component has both hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions. Glycerin (amphipathic—both polar and nonpolar) can mobilize these through polarity matching. The turmeric dye similarly benefits from glycerin's dual-polarity nature. This is why glycerin is ideal—it addresses both components simultaneously without chemical damage risk.
callPro: If glycerin and detergent don't fully resolve mustard, call professional. Silk's delicate nature limits aggressive treatment options.
solventNote: Professional shops: Specialty silk spotting solutions safer than glycerin. Minimal saturation essential. Mustard is two-part stain (fat + dye)—address both components. Do not use bleach or alkaline. Professional expertise prevents fiber damage.
aftercare: Lay flat or hang very loosely. Never wring. As it dries, monitor treated area.
